speaker: interviewer
text: --interview today on the 9th of May, 1995 And this is tape two. So Bronia, you were just telling us about the bombing of Olyka-- Olyka, and your near death experience. Would you like to continue from there on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was very soon after we had to leave Olyka, and-- because my father went back to Lutsk. He went back to Lutsk, and he was elected to the Judenrat. And we went-- we went back to Lutsk. We went to our own unit. We stayed there from about June, July, August-- probably two, three months. And then the-- my-- and then the ghetto was formed. And then we-- we got-- I don't know-- one hours' notice or two hours' notice that-- to take immediate-- you know, whatever we could carry and go to the ghetto. And the ghetto was in a part of Lutsk. There were only a few streets-- only a few streets-- only-- I don't know-- very few houses. Normally, very few people would have lived there. They were next to a medieval castle-- Castle of Lubart-- [NON-ENGLISH], which is still standing there, of course, now. And in those few streets and those few houses, they accommodated nearly 30,000-- 30,000 Jews. Because some Jews, also from the villages nearby, came and stayed in the ghetto, in Lutsk. They had nowhere else to go. There wouldn't have been many of those, but there were some. And we stayed there-- three, four families to a room, sleeping on the floor, on tables, on chairs, getting nine decas of bread-- nine decagrams, which means 90 grams of bread a day-- and practically nothing else. I remember we had some frozen potatoes-- my sister, and my mother, and I, and my father. And we were sharing a kitchen. We were-- we were sharing a room with several other families. And eventually, my father got a little room for only our family in a house-- in a different house. And that's where we stayed until the action in the ghetto, which took place from the 22nd of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. So we were in ghetto for-- from November, December, '41 to the end of August-- 20-something-- August, '42. I was sent out of the ghetto by my father. Oh, my sister went in a-- the [NON-ENGLISH], their [NON-ENGLISH] garden, who was a German, outside the ghetto. Every day, a column of Jewish people left the ghetto. Of course, we all wore the Star-- Star of-- of David, of course, in the front and on the back. In the front and on the back, we wore the Star of David-- [NON-ENGLISH], the yellow-- yellow star. And this column-- there were columns led by Jewish policemen-- Jewish policemen. And Lola, every morning, left-- used to leave that ghetto, and go with the column of people out of the ghetto, and come back in the evening. She worked in that garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in the ghetto, in the hospital. I was the receptionist for the x-ray department. Why did I get the job? Because my best girlfriend's father was a doctor. And he was also in the Judenrat, and he was the director of the Jewish hospital. So he employed me. So I was only letting the people in into the x-ray department. It was all in the ghetto. It was in a Jewish hospital in the ghetto. So you couldn't continue your studies.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- yeah, at this stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God. There were no schools. There was nothing to eat. There was-- there was not a bed to sleep on. There were no clothes. There was nothing. Because we, when we left our apartment, we could only carry one suitcase each. And when we brought the suitcase down, somebody-- we left it on the wrong side of the street, which was not the ghetto. So when we-- we came back to pick up the suitcases, they were gone. So we had nothing-- nothing to wear, nothing. And-- but that didn't bother us. What bothered me, and my sister, and everybody else was the fact that we had no right to live. We had no right to live. For instance, New Year's Eve, in '41, we were there. The ghetto started in November or December. On New Year's Eve, we heard the bells ring in the churches. We heard the people shouting and singing, because we were-- this was a small town. And we were only a few streets. A street away from us were already Christians living, and they were celebrating New Year. We could hear. And we had no right even to-- to leave our apartment into-- it was curfew. We couldn't-- we couldn't even walk in the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- and after, of course, after it got dark, we couldn't even leave the apartments. And a radio or a music-- who was thinking of music? We knew that we-- we knew that we're going to get killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you see, after a few months in the ghetto, they started killing a few people everyday. And the whole of the ghetto knew it. Some of them were killed in the public place right in the ghetto. Some were killed outside of the ghetto. Some of them, like my girlfriend's, [? Tusha ?] Hausman's mother, who was also on the Judenrat, she was a brilliant, wonderful-- she was a great Zionist. She was a friend of Jabotinsky. When he used to come to Lutsk, he used to stay with- with Mrs. Hausman and-- and the family. And she-- when she was shot, she screamed, 'The day will come when you will be revenged. We will be avenged. And you can't get away with these murders.' Of course, she was shot in the middle, you know, where-- where-- before they shot her, she still managed [POINTING] to give them a piece of her mind. So these things happened all the time, and we knew it. So we knew that our turn will come either tomorrow or the day after tomorrow. They were killing the spirit of people. They were killing the spirit. They did-- they wanted to prove to the people that there was no point in fighting. There was nothing to fight about, because we're going to be killed anyway. And my-- my mother contracted typhus, because typus-- there was an epidemic of typhus. My sister and I were immunized against it. My mother was not. And a lot of people were not. My father-- my mother caught it, and she was sick with typhus. It was just before the action took place. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Apropos, what I started telling you-- this, I don't know. But one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I asked you whether you had been able to continue your studies, and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Argh!

speaker: interviewer
text: --showed us that there was no life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who thought of studies?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no life. There was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh! Studies? For God's sakes. This was a luxury. This was something unheard of. There was no right. We had no right to live! We're denied freedom! We couldn't live! We were being killed!

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was thinking of education? That didn't penetrate our-- our thoughts at the time. It was a matter of-- even the survival urge was being killed constantly. I know why I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a letter from a fellow whom I met on the last holiday before the war broke out. And he wrote me a letter. He said, let's-- let's escape together. But he lived in a smaller place. His father also had a flour mill. His name was Ischa Waserman. And he wrote me a letter. Bronia, let's run away together. Let's put up a fight. Around shortly after, I heard that he was already killed. And before he was killed, he-- he-- I think, killed one or two Germans. He fought for his life to the last drop of his blood, before they-- after they killed him. So there was never any chance to run away with him. He was killed in the place where he was. I know why I ask you, or why I mentioned this typhus. People used to stop me in the street, because everyone knew what was happening in there. I mean, there were-- houses are of-- you know, people were living sort of together. In that ghetto, everybody knew what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: People were stopping me in the street asking me, how is your mother today? I used to say to me, you are interested in my mother? You are going to be killed tomorrow. You are interested in my mother's health? You better start looking after yourself! Run away! Do something!

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- because I was aware constantly that we're not going to get-- we alive from that. But I was determined. I was determined that before they kill me-- before they enter the-- the room that I was in, I'm going to boil up some water. I had no weapons. I had nothing, and I wasn't even very strong. I was a young teenager. I was determined that I'm going to fight to the last drop. I was going to boil up some water and pour it over them when they entered the-- the kitchen or the room to get us. I was convinced that I'm going to fight to the last drop of my blood. And anyway, and later on-- well, OK. My father came home and said, tomorrow they need some women and children to-- for a harvest in-- in-- in a village. And I want you and Lola to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Lola went in the morning to get the permission from the [NON-ENGLISH] to-- to leave, and she didn't come in time. So my father gave a signal, and the column took away. And I, and the children, and mothers-- we found-- found ourself in a-- in a small place somewhere-- I don't know-- 30 kilometers away from Lutsk. We walked, of course, by foot, naturally, and slept in a stable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the following day, the Ukrainian guy told me that they're killing the Jews. There's a big holiday in Lutsk. They're killing the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I spread the news. And when I-- after I-- he told me. And when we got to the stable, there was no one there already. In any case, I-- in that field, there was a fellow there who came to collect the straw. There were several, but one came up to me and said, are you Mr. Goldstein's daughter? I said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, I used to deliver timber to your family every winter. We had fireplaces, you see, in-- not open-- closed fireplaces-- in every room. Because the climate there is very harsh. And he used to deliver timber, because there was wood-- wood fireplaces there were. And I said, yes, I'm Mr. Goldstein's daughter. And he said, well, listen. They're going to kill you here. You're going to be killed. You are the next one, you know-- this group here of the Jews. I went-- he wanted to save me. He wanted me to go and live with him and his wife. They were a childless couple. He explained how to get to his house. And I said to him, I am-- I was friendly with a girl who was a bit younger than me-- one or two years younger than me. And I took care of her. And I said, but I'll only go if you take her, too. Well, he wouldn't take her. So in any case, we went the following day to his house-- found the house. We slept in the forest. And there were a few other young people in the forest trying to survive. I don't think any of them survived. I don't think any of them survived. And the next day, he-- I asked him to take the horses and-- and the-- and the carriage, and to drive me and this Hiaca to her sister's place. She had a sister who lived in the country town near Olyka, at the-- which was 57 kilometers from the place that we were at. And he took us there and dropped us in front of the house. And when we knocked on the door, the sister was already dead. There were some other people living in the house. So from then on, we were hiding. We were hiding in-- in-- in stables, and peasants, Ukrainian peasants and Polish peasants, were hiding us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the last time then that you saw your family-- your parents and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw them since I left the ghetto on the 20th or 21st of August, 1941-- '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was when--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were taken on this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have never seen them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except my mother sent me-- the following day, after I left them, somebody else was joining. She sent me a piece of bread and some-- I don't know what was there-- a piece of bread and maybe a tomato. I can't remember what else. She sent me from the ghetto-- the sick woman. They had nothing themselves. But she sent me-- because she thought I might be starving there. It was the next day. Because two days later, they were all killed Anyway, the point is that we hid there. Well, there was-- the ghetto were finished. They-- they eradicated the whole ghetto between the 22nd of August and the 25th or 26th of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard that that the trucks came. And they were loaded on the trucks, taken to a small place three kilometers out of the city-- out of the town, in a little forest, where originally, a few men went to dig the grave. And then they shot them in-- in-- right into the grave. Then they shot everybody from Lutsk right-- you know, on the-- they're bringing them by trucks, shooting them. And they were falling into the graves. And the next truck came, and-- well, I visited this place last year. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So about 30,000 people were killed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think on the grave it states-- I-- I think the figure given on the grave is 27,000-- that 27,000 people lie in this grave. And you should see the size of it-- like this table-- no, bigger-- like this-- this-- well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Small, anyway, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Small-- 27,000 people in one grave. So that-- that happened in August-- August, 1942. And the thing that really makes me very angry is when people now don't mention the eradication of the ghettos. I understand that 1.5-- roughly 1.5 million people were killed in small places, in bigger places, in-- in villages. People were shot just like dead, either in their homes or put on-- on horse and buggy, or a-- a truck, taken out-- or they walked to their deaths. A lot walked to their deaths. And they were shot and put into the graves. There were nearly 1.5 million people in this eastern Poland and Ukraine that never made it to any camp-- never made it to any camp. They killed them right there and then. They didn't bother having trains. They didn't bother running the trains to any concentration camps. They didn't bother getting the gas. They just shot them like-- like lambs! And I get very angry when I hear-- when I don't hear the destruction of the ghettos mentioned on the same level as the camps. It really hurts me, because I myself know thousands of people who were killed that way in eastern Poland and Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can't understand why the emphasis is only on the camps. Of course, millions perished in the camps, but what about the 1.5 to 2 million that perished in the ghettos that never made it to the camps? Why aren't they being honored on the same level as the people in the camps? Because nobody saw them. The Americans didn't arrive there. The Russians came later when they were all killed! They couldn't liberate anybody. Is this the reason that there it's not never mentioned-- that the ghettos-- the destruction of the ghettos is never mentioned? Because nobody saw them being killed? Because there were no survivors to tell on the spot when the Russians entered?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russians were not interested, because they were fighting for their own lives. They were still fighting when they went to-- to Lutsk-- when they liberated Lutsk. They were still-- they still had a long way to go to reach Berlin in '45. We're talking about '44 now when they liberated Lutsk. They were fighting for their own survival. And I saw it. I saw it. Well, I want to talk to you now about what happened when I left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, we should go back to-- to when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We should--

speaker: interviewer
text: --were in hiding-- you started your period of hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a most incredible period, [BACKROUND NOISE] because the most unexpected people hid one. The-- there were simple Ukrainian peasants who couldn't read or write, and didn't even know how to light the-- how to make a fire. If-- I watched a Ukrainian woman cooking, and she used a big pot for a small fire and a small pot for a big fire. It didn't make sense. Their IQ was so low, and yet these people with the low IQ hid Hiaca and me for a few nights in this stable. And another fellow had-- I met two fellows there who were hiding also-- a father and a son-- Volko and Itsak. And they knew-- they were local Jews. They were country Jews. They knew the area. They lived there before the war, so they knew every peasant. And the peasants-- in one or two places-- I bumped into Volko, and I bumped into Itsak. And there was a little brother he had, which I-- whom I never met. And the funny thing is that one day I hid for several days in one stable with Itsak. And he said to me, we must leave. I have a premonition we have to leave. We left it. And that night, they raided this stable and found the little brother there that I never met-- his little brother, [NON-ENGLISH], whom I never laid my-- laid my eyes on. He must have entered that stable that night when Itsak and I left, and they caught him there and killed him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in anyways, this hiding business was terrible. It was terrible, because you-- from moment to moment, you never knew who will-- you know, whether you will be found. For instance, I used to enter haystacks without the-- after Hiaca was killed. She went to get something warm. In November, December, it started getting very cold. This is your friend that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my little friend--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the young-- the young girl-- the young girl-- I was a young girl, too, but she was even younger. And she went to get some coat to wear. She-- she was-- she knew a few people in that area, because she used to come and visit her sister before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went to get an article of clothing. We were freezing in that stable. And she never came back. People recognized her and killed her-- you know, the Jewess and-- she never came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any case, I was hiding there. And most of the time, I was hiding in haystacks-- not most of the time-- some of the time. At night, I went from one haystack to another haystack. Only the dogs knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were on your own at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my-- I was on my own. But I also-- I was on my own, and that was awful. Just imagine lying in a haystack. It was during the day, the peasant putting his-- the-- the thing that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --is like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a fork--

speaker: interviewee
text: --like-- like a

speaker: interviewer
text: A big fork.

speaker: interviewee
text: --fork-- big fork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know how straw carries sound. And you have a choice to make. Either you're going to let him know that you're there so that he doesn't perforate your stomach or your eyes; either you let-- you going to let him know that you're lying there so that he doesn't kill you that way; or if you tell him that you're there, he can straight away start screaming, [NON-ENGLISH] Jewess! Jewess! And somebody will come and knock me dead in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: So out of that choice, I decided not to say anything. And God help me, he didn't-- and, of course, shortly after that when it got dark, I left that haystack. I never went back there. Only dogs and I knew that I was in the haystack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were mice running. And I was scared of mice. And there were mice jumping off my chest, you know, over me. And I'd had to lie there like that. Anyway, there were also some-- there were good people helping me. There was a family, [NON-ENGLISH]. There was a very famous Polish writer called--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this-- just--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- in the same area.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to clarify, all in the same area? Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same area-- the same area around Olyka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what that-- Olyka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around Olyka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 57 kilometers from Lutsk-- between-- between Lutsk and Rovno--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --somewhere there. And there was this-- this writer, Elizabeth Orzeszkowa. And her family lived there. I stayed with them for one month. They risked their own lives-- a Polish family-- on a very big-- I mean, that was a property-- a big property. Here we could call them a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A homestead? Like a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A homestead, yes-- yes, a homestead. What were these people called here? They had the big country houses in Australia when I came to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Graziers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Graziers, yes. They were graziers. And so I was there with them for a month-- for a month. I loved Mrs. Orzeszkowa. She had a son, a-- a student-- a lawyer. And there was-- her-- her husband who was paralyzed, and his brother. Anyway, I stayed there for a month. It was very comfortable. But then I couldn't stay longer, because they were risking their own lives because of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you came to stay with them? Did you know them before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I can't remember now how it happened. Maybe, maybe Itsak took me there, or maybe we just knocked on the-- on the door. I can't remember. Because they have been to so many places, usually for one night, two nights, one day, two days-- never longer than-- than a few nights. But there I stayed for a whole month. And after that-- oh, there were a few Polish households. For instance, where-- where this little [NON-ENGLISH] was killed-- that was a Polish household. They used to even bring us a plate of soup sometimes, you know. But then I-- then-- then-- oh, then a funny thing happened. The Ukrainians started killing the Poles. So all the Polish families that lived there picked up and went to the big cities. They went to Lutsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was hiding in a family there-- a Polish-- with the Polish family. There was Itsak and another Jewish fellow. The three of us were hiding in a stable. But the owners knew. They gave us a plate of soup every night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the Ukrainians started killing the Poles, they had to pick up and disappear, because they would have got killed by the Ukrainian villagers-- peasants. So I decided then that I'm going to go back to Lutsk. And they-- they were on a-- on a coach with the horses. And they had two small children. She had a hunch. She was a hunchback-- the woman. She was a hunchback. And they were on this-- on this coach with the children, with their belongings. And I, like a dog, was running behind that coach. Because they wouldn't have me sit down on the coach.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was running to keep up with that coach. And it was dangerous to be alone in the street, because, you know, either Polish or Jewish-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- the Ukrainians would have killed me. That was Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I was attached to them-- there were a few of us, at least. So I was running to keep up with the coach. And that went on for about 30 kilometers. When we got to Lutsk, I went to-- knocked on the door of my father's friend, the-- the bank manager who already wasn't the bank manager. Because in the meantime, there were Soviets. Now there were Germans. So he was-- the bank-- Polish banks were closed in '39. We're talking now about '40-- '42-- end of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We're talking now-- when did I get back to Lutsk? It was February-- February '43-- in February '43. And I knocked on his door. And they were living in a small little room-- two old people. They were already-- one small room, two single beds, and a-- and a cooker.

speaker: interviewee
text: She immediately got a basin full of water, got a good comb. Made me have a wash, got the-- the things out. I had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: --lice, of course. Well, I never changed my clothes. So she-- I slept in stables, don't forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she-- she cleaned me up, kissed me, fed me. But I couldn't stay with them. But were-- people were calling in. It was under the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would have got shot together with me. That-- had I been seen, because people knew me there. They lived a few houses away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a Polish banker, not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Russian, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Russian-- a Russian from the Czar-- still-- a old Czarist fellow. But he-- so I slept there one night. And he went and told my father's other friend who was a tax-- from the taxation office across the road. He went and told Mr. Schmigero. And they hid me there for-- I don't know how long-- a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long had you been on the run until that time that you returned to Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: From end of August-- from the 20th of August, 1942. I left Lutsk for Rovno on a-- this Mr. Schmigero, who hid me, with his second wife, gave me the identity card of his daughter who was my school friend. She went--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in February '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk-- in Lutsk, in '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, when you-- yeah, when you got back.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '43. He gave me ID of Halinka, his daughter. And-- but first of all, he took me to go another family, because he couldn't hide me any longer. His brother-in-law, who lived in the same house, in a different apartment in the same house, spotted me. And he was married to a gendarme-- a German woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came. And-- oh, I went for a-- for a-- for a plate of soup from this-- from the-- I was lying on top of the house on the straw. What do you call it? Cellar? Not cellar-- cellar's the under-- and attic-- Hay-- oh, the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't an attic. It was straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: A hay-- like the haystack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Haystack-- on top of the house. Anyway, at night--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, on the roof? No, not on the roof.

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the roof--

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the roof-- oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --between the roof and the roofs of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The eaves? Under the eaves?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish-- in Polish, it's called [POLISH]. I don't know how it's called. I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The eaves-- under the eaves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eaves. Right. Anyway, this-- this-- this Czech fellow-- his-- the brother-in-law Mr. Schmigero came and saw me sitting there in the dining room, eating soup. And he came. And he said to me, unless you sign a document that you give all your father's property-- write them over to me-- I am going straight away to the Gestapo, and you're going to be killed right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why am I telling you this story? Of course, I-- I-- I had to leave. The next day, I had to leave. Fortunately, Mr. Scmigero took me to another place, to a solicitor and his wife, who were childless. And that was paradise!

speaker: interviewee
text: That was paradise as I have never experienced it. It was-- this was a childless couple. There was so much love over me. And I gave them so much love. We were so happy together. It was just incredible. It was really the happiest time of my life-- during the war, that is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this still in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Lutsk-- still in Lutsk. In any case-- with that document that Halina [NON-ENGLISH] Scmigero-- now the-- the uprising in Warsaw was in April-- 19th of April, 1943. We-- on the grapevine, we heard about this. And somehow or other, I felt that it wasn't be fair-- that it wasn't fair of me to jeopardize the lives of the people that I loved so much. And if I would have been found there, they would have been shot with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, I said to them, listen. I cannot stay with you any longer. I'm not going to risk your lives for me. I have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that stage-- so I think he brought me the identity card from Schmigero. I don't know how I got it. But I think he may have got it from Scmigero and brought it to me. And he took me to the railway station. And I went to Rovno. Oh, but in the meantime, I got in touch with Danka Corbretska. I had nowhere to go. I didn't know what to do with myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we let know the only Polish girt who-- that I trusted, who was my girlfriend in high school. She came to visit the solicitor, and I emerged from the other room. She nearly collapsed when she saw me, because at that stage, they thought that I was-- that all the Jews were killed. Because that was in August '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Danka and all the Polish people lived in Lutsk. They knew what happened-- that all the Jews were liquidated. And here I appear six months later. Oh, I don't know how many-- August, September, October, November--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --December January, February--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --March whatever. And so she-- April.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said to me, Bronia, yes, I'll help you. My-- I have an-- I have an uncle. He's a priest. He's in Rovno. And I'm going to arrange it. And she did. She did. So she came back and said, yes. My uncle is waiting for you. Just get to Rovno, and he will pick you up from the train on such and such day. It worked. The solicitor took me to the railway station. A chap from the high school recognized me on the station. He said, hello! Aren't you Bronia Goldstein? I said, me? You must be making a mistake. Oh, no, I'm just passing Lutsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid. I didn't know whether to trust him. [BACKROUND NOISE] He could have called the police. He could have started shouting [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, Jews! Jewess! And the people would have shot me on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, you? I? Bronia? No, I'm not Bronia. No, I'm just passing through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the solicitor put me on the train. I finished up in Rovno. And he waited for me-- the priest-- and put me with a chap who was a lion tamer, of all places-- of all things. A widower- with children. And I found a job. I was looking after a German woman and German man. And the-- and it-- the German woman and man lived in a-- in a household where there was a beautiful daughter-- a mother and a beautiful daughter who was a-- her husband went to the army. And she had a German lover-- an old fellow-- an old Austrian. But anyway, one day, I'm in the kitchen. And I was-- I was-- I-- I meant to run away. Because, you see, the Russians were coming closer. We're talking now about '43. It was already summer '43-- summer '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Russians were already pushing the Germans back. Stalingrad was long lost for the-- for the Germans. They were already being chased by the Russian tanks and Russian soldiers. And they were running-- they were sort of going back towards Germany. So I felt that I am going to go towards the Russians. I had the Germans. I had no right to live under the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, in Rovno, I went once through the street, and a group of Jewish men were still alive. A handful of them were led by police, by-- by gestapo. And all of them stared at me. All of them recognized how a Jew recognizes a Jew. I never saw them in my life. I was walking on the-- like the pedestrian walk, and they were walking-- they were marching in a group like cattle on the-- where the cars-- you know, on the-- chaussee-- and all of them staring at me. They recognized. They must have recognized that I was Jewish. Anyway, to cut a long story short, I was in the kitchen. And I was determined to go towards the Russians. And I had my suitcase already from the lion tamer. I already wanted to disappear that day. I wanted to-- to-- to-- to-- to go. So my suitcase with my [NON-ENGLISH], my belongings was already on the-- on the floor there in the-- in the-- the Germans were at work. And then this-- the two rooms that he occupied in that house-- I had my suitcase there on the floor. And I hear a talk from the kitchen. I am in the kitchen preparing something for them to eat, and I hear a talk. The woman-- the beautiful daughter-- tall, beautiful, Russian woman-- beautiful-- saying to the fellow-- to some guy-- some voice I never heard in my life before. He was-- he is saying to her, she is Jewish. She's got to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she is saying to him, what do you want from her? She's a young girl! She's a lovely little girl. She's helping here. She's looking after these two Germans. She's working here. She's never done anybody any harm. Why do you want to kill her?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, she is Jewish! She has to die. I didn't wait for any longer. I just jumped through the window. I ran for my life. I left the suitcase. I ran! He was obviously a gestapo-- private--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Spy.

speaker: interviewee
text: A secret.

speaker: interviewee
text: Secret.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a spy-- secret gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: A secret-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was under the Germans. You see, he was a Ukrainian, you see-- you see-- a Ukrainian working for the gestapo. He was not a German. He was a Ukrainian working for the gestapo. In any case-- or maybe he was a volksdeutche-- or-- I don't know. In any case, I ran. I never saw them again. And I-- I went to this-- to the chaussee, you know, where the traffic was going. And it was already afternoon. And it used to get dark-- no, it was summer. But nevertheless-- ah, there was curfew. You couldn't walk. And don't forget, that was the time when the Ukrainians were killing the Poles. And I had an ID card of a Polish girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was at risk. If I would have been caught alone on the chaussee and a Ukrainian would have come up to me and say, [NON-ENGLISH]-- miss, where are you going, and who are you? If I would say, I'm Halina [NON-ENGLISH] Scmigero-- Polish, they would have chopped up my head off! Because-- We'll-- yeah, we'll-- we'll stop there. All right. Thank you.
